metadata.title: SYNGO_BP_2227
metadata.comment:
Rationale: Background: NO is generated by NO synthase, and exerts most of its biological effects by activation of the heme-containing guanylyl cyclases (NO-GCs), that serves as the primary targets for NO. In response to NO, NO-GCs catalyze the conversion of GTP into cyclic guanosine monophosphate (cGMP) which is an established second messenger molecule involved in neurotransmission and exerts its effects through cGMP-dependent protein kinases, cGMP-regulated phosphodiesterases and ion channels (Feil and Kleppisch, 2008).

Fig.1: "Miniature EPSC frequency is greatly reduced in NO-GC1 KO mice, and depends on cGMP"
Fig.2: "Evoked glutamate release is reduced in NO-GC1 KO mice and greatly depends on cGMP"
Fig.4: "The frequency of miniature IPSCs is strongly reduced in NO-GC1 KO mice, and is mediated by cGMP"
Fig.5: "Evoked GABA release is reduced in NO-GC1 KO mice and greatly dependent on cGMP"
Together: presynaptic modulation of SV release is dependent on NO-GC1

Fig.6: "NO-GC1/cGMP-modulation of GABAergic transmission requires activation of postsynaptic NMDARs."
Postsynaptic blockade of NMDARs prevents the presynaptic change in SV release. This demonstrates the transsynaptic character of the NO transmission.

Note: the 'NO-GC1 KO' gene KO is traced back tot Uniprot protein "Guanylate cyclase soluble subunit alpha-3" for the Gucy1a3 gene via Blat search in USCS browser via sequence 'TCCCGAGATGAAGTAGTTAGTA'. The alternative name for this protein is 'GCS-alpha-1'.

Note: "NO-sensitive GC exists in 2 isoforms containing either an α1 or α2 subunit dimerized to the β1 subunit (α1-GC and α2-GC) with indistinguishable enzymatic properties (9). " Annotation to the obligatory β1 subunit seems in order.. Experimental description: "The generation of NO-GC1 KO mice has been described previously..."
